summary: Jeff Atwood explores alternative keyboard input devices, prompted by a Tom's Hardware review of the Ergodex DX1. He reflects on the limitations of the standard QWERTY keyboard, which has remained essentially unchanged for 138 years despite technological advances. While showcasing various alternative input peripherals he's tried, Atwood argues that these devices work best as complements to traditional keyboards rather than replacements. He attributes this to the nearly insurmountable challenge of overcoming ingrained muscle memory, the same problem that has plagued the Dvorak keyboard layout.
key_points: The standard QWERTY keyboard has remained the dominant input device for 138 years despite its limitations
Alternative input devices like gaming keypads and customizable keyboards have consistently failed to replace traditional keyboards
Muscle memory from years of keyboard use creates an almost insurmountable barrier to adopting radically different input methods
The most practical approach for alternative input devices is to complement existing keyboards rather than replace them
Flexibility and customization, as offered by the Ergodex DX1, may be the key to making alternative input devices actually useful
money_quotes: The struggle to defeat a decade of existing keyboard muscle memory is a losing battle
So far, technology hasn't provided us with the ability to customize a hardware interface that meets our individual needs
Thus, we are usually forced to retreat to the 138-year-old quintessence of ubiquity: Christopher Sholes' 'QWERTY' keyboard
These keyboard alternatives don't replace but complement your existing keyboard by presenting an optimized subset of what you already know
It's really the same problem the Dvorak keyboard layout has always had
key_insight: Alternative input devices succeed not by trying to replace the keyboard entirely, but by complementing it with optimized, specialized controls that work alongside ingrained muscle memory.
